items.q: What three Ps are added to make the extended marketing mix (7Ps)?
items.correct: People (staff/service quality), Process (how the service is delivered), and Physical Evidence (tangible cues — uniforms, packaging, branding) — added to make the 4Ps relevant to services marketing.
items.misconception: The 7Ps are just the 4Ps repeated with different words.
items.random_wrong: The 7Ps include Profit, Planning and Performance.
items.why: The extended mix specifically addresses services, where customer experience depends on staff, process and atmospherics — not just product. Students treat 4Ps and 7Ps as interchangeable, missing what services marketing requires.

items.q: What is a brand in marketing?
items.correct: A combination of name, design, symbols and associations that distinguishes a product and creates customer perception and loyalty — more than just a logo.
items.misconception: A brand is just the logo on a product.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the company that makes a product.
items.why: Branding is the sum of perceptions, not just visual identity. Coca-Cola the brand is much more than the script logo — it is associations, taste expectations, advertising history.

items.q: What is operations management concerned with?
items.correct: The design and control of the production process and business operations — converting inputs into outputs efficiently. It includes capacity, quality, supply chain, and process improvement.
items.misconception: Operations management is the same as general management.
items.random_wrong: Operations management is only about manufacturing factories.
items.why: Operations is a specialised function focused on the production process; it applies equally to services (hospitals, airlines). Students treat it as factory-only or as general management, missing the distinctive process-focused discipline.

items.q: The Boston Matrix was developed by:
items.why: It's BCG. “McKinsey” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Selling new products to existing customers (Ansoff) is:
items.why: Correct answer: Product development. “Market penetration” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Ansoff publish his growth matrix?
items.why: Exact answer: 1957 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
